name: Tupac Hologram
description: The Tupac Hologram theory centers around the belief that the performance featuring a likeness of Tupac Shakur at the 2012 Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival was not a genuine hologram, but rather a combination of Pepper's Ghost illusion, CGI, and potentially even a body double. Proponents of this theory argue that the technology available at the time was not sophisticated enough to create a true holographic projection of that complexity and realism. They cite inconsistencies in the performance's lighting, movement, and overall aesthetic as evidence. Furthermore, some believe that the technology was used as a way to gauge public reaction to the idea of posthumous performances of deceased artists and potentially even to perpetuate the idea that Tupac is still alive and working secretly. The mainstream perspective is that the performance was indeed a sophisticated illusion leveraging existing technology, primarily Pepper's Ghost combined with advanced projection mapping and CGI enhancements. Experts in visual effects and stagecraft have explained the technical feasibility of creating such an illusion using the described methods, emphasizing that it doesn't require futuristic or nonexistent holographic technology. Academic sources generally treat the phenomenon as a case study in spectacle, digital resurrection, and the evolving relationship between audiences and deceased performers. Critics point out that the 'hologram' was never advertised as a true hologram, and the term was used loosely in media coverage. The theory is fueled by the broader 'Tupac is alive' conspiracy, adding another layer of intrigue to the late rapper's legacy.
subtitle: Digital Resurrection and Conspiracy Theories

claims.claim: The 2012 Coachella performance was not a true hologram.
claims.evidence: Technical limitations of holographic technology at the time, inconsistencies in the projection's appearance.
claims.criticism: The performance utilized existing illusion techniques like Pepper's Ghost and projection mapping, enhanced with CGI. It was never presented as a true hologram.

claims.claim: The performance was a test to gauge public reaction to posthumous performances.
claims.evidence: The novelty of the performance and the ensuing media frenzy.
claims.criticism: Posthumous performances were already common through audio and video recordings. The Coachella performance was primarily a spectacle designed for entertainment.

claims.claim: The 'hologram' was used to perpetuate the 'Tupac is alive' conspiracy.
claims.evidence: The enduring popularity of the conspiracy and the performance's timing.
claims.criticism: The performance simply capitalized on Tupac's iconic status and fueled existing fan interest. No credible evidence links it to any scheme to fake his death.

keyFigures.name: Dr. Dre
keyFigures.role: Executive producer involved in the performance

keyFigures.name: Ed Ulbrich
keyFigures.role: Chief Creative Officer of Digital Domain, the company that created the illusion

keyFigures.name: Numerous online conspiracy theorists
keyFigures.role: Promoting and elaborating the hologram conspiracy theory

timeline.event: Proliferation of the Tupac Hologram conspiracy theory online
